text:
Day nine of theParis2024Olympicswas another memorable one, with Noah Lyles stealing the headlines after aremarkable final in the men’s 100m at the Stade de France.

Despite appearing out of the contest with 50m remaining, the American superstar clawed his way back to beat Jamaica’s Kishane Thompson and fellow countryman Fred Kerley, who claimed silver and bronze respectively.

Elsewhere, there was heartbreak for Great Britain’s Amber Rutter, who was controversiallybeaten by Chile’s Francisca Crovetto in a shoot-outafter the women’s skeet shooting final had finished all level.

Lottie Fry picked up her second medal of the Olympics by taking bronze in the Individual Dressage, whilst Harry Hepworth became the first British man to win a medal in the vault by claiming bronze.

Novak Djokovic, meanwhile, has now completed the Golden Slam, with the Serbian tennis legendbeating Carlos Alcaraz in the men’s finalto claim the only major honour that had evaded him in his illustrious career.

This blog is now closed. For the latest updates, visitMetro’s Sport page.

Another unforgettable day at the Olympic Games in Paris is over.

Join us here atMetro.co.ukbright and early in the morning for yet more drama, with day 10 set to be a thriller.

Convicted rapist Steven van de Veldebroke down in tears after he was knocked out of the men’s beach volleyball competition earlier this evening.

Van de Velde, competing alongside fellow Dutchman Matthew Immers, suffered a straight-sets defeat to Brazil’s Evandro Oliveira and Arthur Diego Mariano Lanci, bringing their campaign to an end at the round-of-16 stage.

The 26-year-old was convicted for the rape of a 12-year-old English girl in 2016 – when he was aged 19 – and his participation at the Paris Games has proved hugely divisive.

Read thefull story here.

Noah Lyles, the newly crowned men’s 100m Olympic champion, says he thought Kishane Thompson had won their final.

‘Me and Kishane were at the end waiting for our names to come up and I came back and I said, “I’ll be honest, man, I think you got that one”,’ Lyles told SportsMax TV after clinching gold.

‘But my name came up and I was like, “Oh my gosh, I’m amazing!”.’

Read thefull story here.

Olympic organisers at this summer’s Paris Games have come in for heavy criticism after the athletes involved in the men’s 100m final were made to wait an age for the start of the race.

Steve Cram, Colin Jackson and Denise Lewis were among those to hit out at the nervy wait the competitors faced at the Stade de France.

‘This is the finite margins we’re talking about and that for me was just way too long,’ former British heptathlete Lewis said in the BBC Sport studio.

Read thefull story here.

It doesn’t get much closer than that…

Despite appearing out of the contest with 50 metres remaining of the men’s 100m final, Noah Lyles clawed his way back to beat Jamaica’s Kishane Thompson and fellow countryman Fred Kerley, who claimed silver and bronze respectively.

Remarkably, just five thousandths of a second separated the American and Thompson in a sensational photo finish.

Read thefull story here.

Noah Lyles is the new 100m Olympic champion !

In a pulsating race at the Stade de France, the US superstar appeared out of the contest before storming home in a time of 9.79 – winning by five thousandths of a second.

Jamaica’s Kishane Thompson and Fred Kerley of the United States finished second and third to claim the silver and bronze respectively.

Simply stunning.

Ethan Katzberg has written his name into the history books, securing gold for Canada in the men’s hammer throw final.

The 22-year-old’s opening effort of 84.12m went unbeaten through all six rounds at the Stade de France.

Hungary’s Bence Halasz claims the silver medal, while Mykhaylo Kokhan of Ukraine takes bronze.

Yaroslava Mahuchikh got her hands on Ukraine’s second gold medal of the Paris Olympics with victory in the women’s high jump final.

Mahuchikh saw off fierce competition for Australia’s Nicola Olyslagers to seal the top prize, Ukraine’s fifth medal overall in the French capital this summer.

Her compatriot, Iryna Gerashchenko, shares the bronze medal with Eleanor Patterson of Australia.

Neil Gourley ensured he will join Josh Kerr in the final of the men’s 1500m after a running an impressive race in his semi-final.

The Team GB star came third behind Yared Nuguse and Steven Nessler of the United States, who finished first and second respectively.

Gourley, a three-time national champion, is the British indoor record holder for the 1500m.

Janjaem Suwannapheng says she is ‘not afraid’ of Imane Khelif ahead of their semi-final meeting in the women’s boxing at theParisOlympics.

The Thai fighter advanced from her 66kg quarter-final against Turkey’s Busenaz Surmeneli to set up a bout against Khelif.

It comes after Algeria’s Khelif was disqualified from last year’s Women’s World Championships for failing International Boxing Association (IBA)gendereligibility rules.

Read thefull story here.

Following on from Keely Hodgkinson’s impressive showing, Josh Kerr booked his place in the final of the men’s 1500m.

The hotly tipped Team GB star finished behind Norway’s Jacob Ingebrigtsen in his semi-final, with the pair shoulder to shoulder for much of the race.

Fellow Brit George Mills missed out on Tuesday’s final after finishing down in 11th.

Team GB needed a little boost at the Stade de France and Keely Hodgkinson has provided it.

She’s the favourite to win the 800m event and makes light work of her semi-final to progress to the showpiece event, which takes place on Monday night.

Jemma Reekie was well-placed with 100m to go but looked out on her feet towards the winning line and dropped to fifth.

She looks dejected as well as exhausted.

Well it’s been a bad few minutes for Team GB at the Stade de France, with Zharnel Hughes following Louis Hinchliffe in exiting the 100m competition.

Kishane Thompson cruises to victory to advance to the final.

‘Obviously I’m disappointed but I know where I am coming from,’ Hughes said. ‘This is my third 100m. Not making any excuses. Today just wasn’t my day.

‘To try to get through the rounds of the Olympics after injuring my hamstring with a grade two tear last month wasn’t that easy but I dug deep to give myself a try and that’s what I came here to do.

‘I don’t have to focus on the 100m anymore, just focus on the 200m and I’ll be fine.’

Results in the next 100m semi-final mean Great Britain’s Louie Hinchliffe won’t be in the 100m final later on this evening.

Allison Curbishley, former GB 400m runner, said on BBC Radio 5 Live: ‘I feel for Louie Hinchliffe.

‘He is walking through the mixed zone with his head down, it shouldn’t be down. He made an Olympic semi-final and almost made an Olympic final. He should be proud.’

Jamaica’s Oblique Seville makes every yard to win the semi-final, even taunting his rivals by looking across as he hits the winning line.

Team GB’s Louie Hinchcliffe recovered from a sluggish start to finish third in 9.97 seconds, meaning he faces an anxious wait to see if he qualifies for the final.

The semi-finals of the men’s 100m sprint are coming up and Great Britain’s Louie Hinchliffe is involved.

Can Sheffield’s fastest man reach tonight’s 100m final?

In the women’s event, it’s Team USA who swim away from their rivals, shattering their own world record to take gold in the women’s 4x100m medley relay final.

Australia finish second with China back in third.

description: Latest news and live updates from 2024 Paris Olympic Games on day 9 (August 4) schedule: Athletics, gymnastics, swimming, golf and boxing.
